title: Chiefs unveil renderings of planned $3B domed stadium in Kansas
dek: The Kansas City Chiefs released renderings for a $3 billion domed stadium scheduled to open for the 2031 NFL season in Wyandotte County, Kansas. The 70,000-seat facility will feature a translucent ceiling designed to amplify crowd noise and a hall of honor twice the size of Arrowhead Stadium's. Kansas lawmakers approved STAR bonds covering up to 70% of the project cost in late December.
author: The GoCheckMySports Desk
key_fact: The Chiefs unveiled renderings for a $3 billion domed stadium set to open in 2031, with a translucent ethylene tetrafluoroethylene ceiling and capacity for 70,000 fans.
bottom_line: Construction details and the exact site location remain to be determined as the project advances toward a 2031 opening. The success of the facility in attracting major events beyond regular NFL games will depend on how Kansas City positions itself in competition with other new NFL stadiums currently under development across the country.
body: The Kansas City Chiefs unveiled renderings on Tuesday for a $3 billion domed stadium scheduled to open for the 2031 NFL season, anchoring a broader mixed-use development in Wyandotte County, Kansas, near the Kansas Speedway and The Legends retail district.

The facility will hold 70,000 seats and feature a translucent ethylene tetrafluoroethylene ceiling (a lightweight polymer material) designed to allow natural light while amplifying crowd noise, addressing season-ticket member requests to preserve the sound environment that made Arrowhead Stadium the Guinness world record holder for loudest stadium with fans generating 142.2 decibels in 2014. The dome will include a seating bowl reminiscent of Arrowhead, with the Chiefs' ring of honor placed above the bowl and a hall of honor twice the size of the current one. The team store will be four times larger than the present venue.

Kansas lawmakers voted unanimously in late December to approve STAR bonds, which will cover up to 70% of the stadium and accompanying mixed-use district costs, according to ESPN's reporting. Clark Hunt and his family committed an additional $1 billion in development, a portion of which will be incentivized by the STAR bond program. The stadium will be surrounded by more than 20,000 parking spots.

The architectural firm MANICA, based in Kansas City, Kansas, designed the structure with a symmetrical and curved exterior, including a scalloped upper deck in each end zone that mirrors Arrowhead's design. Hunt said during the NFL draft that the field will be designed so the Chiefs will not need to expand its width for World Cup matches, a step required at Arrowhead. Hunt stated the stadium will serve the community well for a minimum of 30 years and hopefully closer to 60 years.

The Chiefs plan to bid on hosting major sporting events starting in 2032, per Hunt's statement. Those events include Final Fours, College Football Playoff games, bowl games, and a Super Bowl. The new facility is also expected to increase the Chiefs' luxury suites and other premium amenities to boost franchise revenue.

Arrowhead Stadium, which opened in 1972, will remain in use until the new stadium is complete. The specific site within Wyandotte County has not yet been finalized, according to ESPN's reporting, despite Gov. Laura Kelly's December announcement that the stadium will be located in the county. The area proposed for the stadium is also home to Children's Mercy Park, the home of Major League Soccer club Sporting Kansas City.
